question: A Korean bank with EU operations deploys an AI credit scoring system for retail loan applicants in Germany. The AI Review Committee must determine whether this system qualifies as High-Risk AI under the EU AI Act. What classification rules apply?
ground_truth: Under EU AI Act Article 6(2), AI systems listed in Annex III are classified as High-Risk. Annex III §5(b) explicitly lists 'AI systems intended to be used to evaluate the creditworthiness of natural persons or establish their credit score, with the exception of AI systems used for the purpose of detecting financial fraud' as High-Risk AI. Therefore: (1) The credit scoring AI is definitively High-Risk under Annex III §5(b) — this is not a borderline case; EU legislature explicitly identified creditworthiness evaluation as a domain warranting High-Risk classification due to its potential to limit access to essential financial services; (2) Fraud detection exception — Article explicitly carves out fraud detection AI from this High-Risk classification; the bank's credit scoring AI does not qualify for this exception; (3) Practical implications — as a High-Risk AI system, the credit scoring AI is subject to all Chapter III Section 2 requirements: risk management (Art.9), data governance (Art.10), technical documentation (Art.11), record-keeping (Art.12), transparency to deployers (Art.13), human oversight (Art.14), accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity (Art.15); (4) Provider vs Deployer — if the bank develops the AI internally, it is the 'provider' subject to Chapter III Section 3 obligations; if using a vendor's AI, the bank is the 'deployer' subject to Article 26 obligations; (5) Timeline — High-Risk AI obligations under Chapter III were applicable from August 2026 per Article 113.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Annex III §5(b); Article 6(2); Chapter III
notes: Annex III §5(b)의 명시적 분류; 가장 기본적인 EU AI Act 금융 분류 질문

question: The bank's insurance subsidiary uses an AI system for life and health insurance risk assessment and premium pricing. The AI Review Committee asks whether this qualifies as High-Risk AI under the EU AI Act.
ground_truth: Annex III §5(c) explicitly classifies 'AI systems intended to be used for risk assessment and pricing in relation to natural persons in the case of life and health insurance' as High-Risk AI. This is one of the clearest classifications under Annex III: (1) Definitive High-Risk classification — life and health insurance pricing AI is explicitly listed; no ambiguity exists for the committee; (2) Rationale — the EU legislature identified insurance pricing AI as High-Risk because algorithmic pricing can create barriers to essential health and life protection, potentially based on proxies for protected characteristics (health conditions, disabilities, genetic factors); (3) Scope clarity — the provision specifically covers life (생명보험) and health insurance (건강보험) pricing; property and casualty insurance pricing AI is not explicitly listed in Annex III §5(c) but may be assessed under other categories; (4) Full Chapter III Section 2 obligations apply — the insurance pricing AI must implement: Art.9 risk management system; Art.10 data and data governance; Art.11 technical documentation; Art.12 logging; Art.13 transparency to deployers; Art.14 human oversight; Art.15 accuracy and robustness; (5) Article 26 deployer obligations — the insurance subsidiary as deployer must: conduct fundamental rights impact assessment (Art.27); implement human oversight; use AI only for intended purpose; (6) Note the parallel with Korean AI기본법 — while Korean law does not explicitly list insurance pricing as 고영향 AI, EU AI Act's explicit classification provides a strong basis for treating it as 고영향 AI under §2(4)(사) or (카).
legal_basis: EU AI Act Annex III §5(c); Article 6(2)
notes: Annex III §5(c) 명시적 분류; 생명·건강보험 가격 산정 AI

question: The AI Review Committee must implement an Article 9 Risk Management System for the credit scoring High-Risk AI. What are the specific requirements of this system, and how does it differ from a generic risk management approach?
ground_truth: Article 9 of the EU AI Act requires a comprehensive Risk Management System (RMS) for High-Risk AI systems with the following specific characteristics: (1) Continuous iterative process (Art.9(1)-(2)) — the RMS must be established, implemented, documented, and maintained throughout the entire lifecycle of the High-Risk AI system; it is not a one-time exercise but a continuous iterative process requiring regular systematic review and updating; (2) Four mandatory steps (Art.9(2)): (a) identification and analysis of known and reasonably foreseeable risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights; (b) estimation and evaluation of risks during intended use AND under reasonably foreseeable misuse; (c) evaluation of other risks from post-market monitoring data (Art.72); (d) adoption of appropriate and targeted risk management measures; (3) Residual risk acceptability (Art.9(5)) — risk management measures must ensure that residual risk associated with each hazard is acceptable, and overall residual risk is acceptable; (4) Testing requirement (Art.9(6)-(8)) — High-Risk AI must be tested against pre-defined metrics and probabilistic thresholds appropriate to its intended purpose; testing must occur throughout development and must be completed before market placement; (5) Vulnerable population consideration (Art.9(9)) — providers must consider whether the AI is likely to have adverse impact on persons under 18 or other vulnerable groups; (6) Integration with existing financial risk frameworks (Art.9(10)) — for financial institutions already subject to Union law risk management requirements (e.g., CRD risk management, Solvency II), Article 9 requirements may be part of or combined with those existing procedures; this is the EU AI Act's equivalence provision, paralleling Korean AI기본법 §34(3).
notes: Art.9 리스크 관리 시스템; 금융 기존 리스크 프레임워크와 통합

question: Article 9(10) of the EU AI Act allows financial institutions' AI risk management to be integrated with existing internal risk management processes under Union law. How should the AI Review Committee leverage this provision to avoid duplicative compliance?
ground_truth: Article 9(10) is a critical efficiency provision for financial institutions: 'For providers of high-risk AI systems that are subject to requirements regarding internal risk management processes under other relevant provisions of Union law, the aspects provided in paragraphs 1 to 9 may be part of, or combined with, the risk management procedures established pursuant to that law.' For a Korean bank with EU operations: (1) Applicable Union law frameworks — financial institutions are subject to multiple Union risk management requirements: CRD VI/CRR III (credit institution risk management), Solvency II (insurance), DORA (digital operational resilience), MiFID II (investment firm risk management); (2) Integration opportunity — the credit scoring AI's risk management can be integrated into the institution's existing CRD/IRB model risk management framework rather than creating a separate Article 9 RMS; the key is that the existing framework must actually cover all Article 9 requirements (lifecycle coverage, testing, residual risk management, post-market monitoring); (3) Gap analysis requirement — the AI Review Committee must conduct a gap analysis: which Article 9 requirements are covered by existing processes? Which are genuinely novel? Common gaps: Art.9(9) vulnerable population consideration is typically not in financial model risk management; Art.9(2)(c) post-market monitoring integration; (4) Documentation of integration — the integration approach must be documented; regulators may request evidence that the combined approach satisfies all Article 9 requirements; (5) Practical benefit — integration reduces compliance overhead and aligns AI risk management with the institution's existing risk governance structure, improving rather than fragmenting risk oversight.
notes: 금융 기존 리스크 관리와 통합; 중복 규제 회피 핵심 조항

question: Article 10 of the EU AI Act imposes data governance requirements for High-Risk AI. What specific data obligations apply to the credit scoring AI's training and validation datasets?
ground_truth: Article 10 of the EU AI Act establishes comprehensive data governance requirements for High-Risk AI training, validation, and testing datasets: (1) Appropriate data management practices (Art.10(1)) — training, validation, and testing data must be subject to appropriate data management and governance practices; for credit scoring AI, this includes documenting data sources, collection methodologies, and preprocessing steps; (2) Relevance, representativeness, and absence of errors (Art.10(2)) — datasets must be relevant, sufficiently representative, and to the best extent possible, free of errors and complete in view of the intended purpose; for credit scoring AI: data must represent the actual population of loan applicants (not just historically approved borrowers); must include adequate representation of demographic groups to enable fairness validation; must be reviewed for data entry errors, outdated records, and systematic collection biases; (3) Examination for biases (Art.10(2)(f)) — datasets must be examined for possible biases that could affect fundamental rights or discrimination; for credit AI, this requires explicit bias auditing of training data against demographic characteristics; (4) Special category data limitation (Art.10(5)) — processing of special categories of personal data (race, ethnic origin, political opinions, religion, health data) is only permitted when strictly necessary for detecting and correcting bias, with appropriate safeguards; (5) Data retention for documentation (Art.11) — data documentation must be retained in the technical documentation; (6) GDPR interaction — data governance under Article 10 must be consistent with GDPR requirements; purpose limitation, data minimization, and accuracy principles apply; (7) The AI Review Committee must verify data governance compliance as a pre-deployment requirement.
notes: 데이터 거버넌스; 훈련 데이터의 대표성과 편향 검사

question: The AI Review Committee discovers that the credit scoring AI's training data underrepresents women and ethnic minority applicants. How does Article 10 of the EU AI Act require the committee to address this data bias?
ground_truth: Article 10(2)(f) requires examination of datasets for possible biases that could lead to risks to health, safety, fundamental rights, or discrimination. Article 10(5) provides a narrow authorization for processing special category data (including ethnic origin) to detect and correct bias. For underrepresentation of women and ethnic minorities: (1) Mandatory bias examination — the underrepresentation is a known bias that must be documented and addressed before deployment; underrepresentation means the model is trained primarily on patterns from majority groups and may generalize poorly to underrepresented groups; (2) Impact assessment — quantify the effect of underrepresentation: compare model performance (accuracy, fairness metrics) across demographic groups; identify whether underrepresented groups receive systematically worse credit outcomes than their actual creditworthiness would justify; (3) Remediation options: data augmentation — collect additional representative data from underrepresented groups (requires GDPR-compliant data collection); reweighting — adjust training sample weights to increase relative representation of underrepresented groups; fairness constraints — implement algorithmic fairness constraints during model training; (4) Special category data processing (Art.10(5)) — if ethnic origin data is needed to detect and correct bias, it may be processed under strict conditions: strictly necessary for bias correction; appropriate safeguards for fundamental rights; ethnic data cannot be used for any other purpose; (5) Residual risk documentation — if full bias correction is not achievable, document residual disparities and their justification in the technical documentation (Art.11); (6) The AI Review Committee should make bias remediation a condition of deployment approval.
notes: 훈련 데이터 편향 수정; 특수 범주 데이터 처리 조건

question: Article 13 of the EU AI Act requires providers of High-Risk AI to ensure transparency and provision of information to deployers. If the credit scoring AI is provided by a fintech vendor to the bank, what information must the vendor provide?
ground_truth: Article 13 requires that High-Risk AI systems are designed and developed to be sufficiently transparent to allow deployers to interpret the system's output and use it appropriately. For a fintech vendor (provider) delivering credit scoring AI to a bank (deployer): (1) Instructions for use (Art.13(1)) — providers must supply High-Risk AI systems with instructions for use that enable deployers to implement the human oversight measures required under Article 14; (2) Required information content (Art.13(3)) — the instructions must include: (a) identity and contact details of the provider; (b) High-Risk AI system characteristics, capabilities, and limitations including accuracy performance and performance variations across populations; (c) intended purpose of the AI system; (d) level of accuracy including metrics, robustness measures, and relevant standards used to measure it; (e) known and foreseeable circumstances that may lead to risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights; (f) performance levels in relation to specific persons or groups; (g) human oversight measures including technical limitations of automation; (h) expected lifetime of the system and maintenance updates; (3) Deployer-specific relevance — the bank (as deployer) needs this information to: implement Article 26 deployer obligations; design appropriate human oversight per Art.14; conduct the fundamental rights impact assessment per Art.27; (4) AI Review Committee role — verify that the vendor provides complete Article 13 instructions before accepting any High-Risk AI; incomplete instructions are a grounds for rejecting or renegotiating the vendor relationship.
notes: Art.13 배포자에 대한 투명성; 공급자-배포자 정보 제공

question: Article 14 of the EU AI Act mandates human oversight for High-Risk AI. What specific human oversight measures must the AI Review Committee implement for the credit scoring AI?
ground_truth: Article 14 requires that High-Risk AI systems are designed and developed to enable effective human oversight during their use. The oversight measures must be built-in where technically feasible. Specific requirements: (1) Understanding and monitoring capability (Art.14(4)(a)) — designated persons must be able to understand the High-Risk AI system's capabilities and limitations, and duly monitor its operation to detect and address signs of anomalies, dysfunctions, and unexpected performance; (2) Awareness of automation bias (Art.14(4)(b)) — overseers must be aware of the tendency to automatically rely on or over-rely on the AI output (automation bias); loan officers must be trained to critically evaluate AI credit recommendations; (3) Interpretation capability (Art.14(4)(c)) — overseers must be able to correctly interpret High-Risk AI output; for credit scoring AI, this means loan officers must understand what the AI score means, what factors drive it, and when the score may be unreliable; (4) Override ability (Art.14(4)(d)) — overseers must be able to decide not to use the High-Risk AI system or to override or disregard its output; the bank's loan officers must have explicit authority and a defined process to override AI credit recommendations; (5) Intervention capability (Art.14(4)(e)) — for High-Risk AI with significant safety implications, the ability to intervene or interrupt through a stop button or similar procedure; (6) Implementation for credit AI — the AI Review Committee must ensure: documented role of human loan officer in the credit process; training program on AI capabilities and limitations; override logging requirements; regular review of override patterns to assess oversight effectiveness.
notes: Art.14 인간 감독; 5가지 구체적 요건

question: Article 11 requires providers of High-Risk AI to prepare technical documentation before market placement. What must this documentation contain for the credit scoring AI, and who is responsible for maintaining it?
ground_truth: Article 11 requires that technical documentation for High-Risk AI is drawn up before the system is placed on the market or put into service, and is kept up to date. The documentation must demonstrate that the High-Risk AI meets all Chapter III Section 2 requirements. Annex IV specifies the minimum content: (1) General description of the AI system — including intended purpose, version information, how the AI system interacts with hardware/software, and how it is made available; (2) Detailed description of system elements — the logic, algorithms, and data (training methodology, training data sets, data preprocessing, training assumptions and objectives); (3) Monitoring, functioning and control — information on technical capabilities including accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity performance; (4) Risk management system — description of the Art.9 risk management system; (5) Changes throughout lifecycle — description of any changes made post-market; (6) General information on validation and testing — results of accuracy and performance measurements, test protocols, and test samples; (7) Standards applied — list of harmonised standards applied; (8) EU Declaration of Conformity — copy of the declaration; (9) Post-market monitoring system — description of the system per Art.72; Responsibility allocation: (a) If the bank is the provider (develops internally) — the bank prepares and maintains all technical documentation; (b) If the bank is the deployer (uses vendor AI) — the vendor (provider) is responsible for Art.11 technical documentation; the bank must verify this documentation exists and request access for regulatory examination purposes; (c) The AI Review Committee should require technical documentation before granting approval for any High-Risk AI deployment.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Article 11; Annex IV
notes: Art.11 기술 문서; Annex IV 최소 내용 요건

question: Article 12 of the EU AI Act requires automatic logging of events in High-Risk AI systems. What logging requirements must the AI Review Committee implement for the credit scoring AI?
ground_truth: Article 12 requires that High-Risk AI systems are technically capable of keeping records of events ('logs') automatically throughout their operational lifetime. The logging requirements serve accountability and post-incident analysis purposes: (1) Automated logging capability (Art.12(1)) — the credit scoring AI must be technically designed to generate logs automatically; manual logging alone does not satisfy this requirement; (2) Minimum log contents (Art.12(2)) — logs must enable at minimum: identification of time period of each use of the system; reference database against which the input data was checked (if applicable); input data that led to a particular output; identification of natural persons involved in verification; (3) Traceability requirement — logs must be sufficient to enable post-hoc reconstruction of individual credit decisions, including the input data, model version, and output; this supports the fundamental rights impact assessment (Art.27) and customer appeals; (4) Retention period — logs should be retained for a period appropriate to the AI system's intended purpose and applicable legal obligations; for credit decisions, align with financial record-keeping requirements (minimum 5-7 years); (5) Deployer vs Provider obligation — where the deployer (bank) does not have control over the logging system, Article 12 requires the provider (vendor) to implement logging; the bank must contractually require adequate logging from vendors; (6) Connection to explainability — Article 12 logs provide the evidentiary basis for explaining individual credit decisions to customers upon request; the AI Review Committee must verify logging is implemented before deployment approval.
notes: Art.12 자동 로깅; 신용 AI 의사결정 추적 가능성

question: If the bank develops a credit scoring AI internally and places it on the market as a product sold to other financial institutions, what obligations does Article 16 of the EU AI Act impose on the bank as a 'provider'?
ground_truth: Article 16 establishes the full range of obligations for providers of High-Risk AI systems. If the bank develops credit scoring AI and provides it to other financial institutions, as a provider it must: (1) Ensure compliance with Chapter III Section 2 requirements (Art.16(a)) — implement and document all of: risk management system (Art.9); data governance (Art.10); technical documentation (Art.11); logging capability (Art.12); transparency to deployers (Art.13); human oversight design (Art.14); accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity (Art.15); (2) Quality Management System (Art.16(b)) — implement an Article 17 QMS covering all provider obligations; (3) Technical documentation (Art.16(c)) — maintain Annex IV technical documentation; (4) Conformity assessment (Art.16(d)) — where required, undergo the conformity assessment procedure under Article 43; (5) Registration (Art.16(e)) — register the High-Risk AI system in the EU database per Article 49; (6) Corrective action obligation (Art.16(f)) — take corrective action if the system does not conform to requirements, including withdrawal from market if necessary; notify authorities and deployers; (7) Post-market monitoring (Art.16(g)) — establish and implement Article 72 post-market monitoring plan; (8) Cooperation with authorities (Art.16(h)) — cooperate with national competent authorities upon request; (9) Authorised representative (Art.16(i)) — if established outside the EU, designate an authorised representative per Article 22; (10) The AI Review Committee must ensure all Article 16 obligations are fulfilled before the AI system is provided to any other institution.
notes: Art.16 공급자 의무 전체 목록; 내부 개발 AI를 외부 공급 시

question: Article 17 requires High-Risk AI providers to implement a Quality Management System (QMS). What must this QMS include for a financial institution providing credit scoring AI?
ground_truth: Article 17(1) requires providers to put in place a Quality Management System ensuring compliance with the EU AI Act. The QMS must document policies, procedures, instructions, and processes covering: (1) Regulatory compliance strategy (Art.17(1)(a)) — the strategy for compliance with applicable regulations, including how the provider monitors regulatory changes; (2) Design and development procedures (Art.17(1)(b)) — examination, testing, and validation procedures for the AI system; (3) Monitoring, reporting, investigation procedures (Art.17(1)(c)) — including post-market monitoring per Art.72 and serious incident reporting per Art.73; (4) Data management procedures (Art.17(1)(d)) — collection, examination, retention, and data management consistent with Art.10; (5) Risk management system (Art.17(1)(e)) — reference to the Art.9 RMS; (6) Post-market monitoring system (Art.17(1)(f)) — reference to the Art.72 system; (7) Communication procedures (Art.17(1)(g)) — procedures for communicating with competent authorities, other operators, customers, and users; (8) Record-keeping procedures (Art.17(1)(h)) — documentation of all decisions and activities; (9) Resource management (Art.17(1)(i)) — including competency management; (10) Accountability framework (Art.17(1)(j)) — responsibilities of management; Article 17(3) — for financial institutions already subject to financial regulation quality management requirements (e.g., ISO 9001, banking QMS standards), these may form the basis for the EU AI Act QMS with AI-specific additions. The AI Review Committee should evaluate whether existing QMS frameworks can be extended to cover EU AI Act requirements.
notes: Art.17 품질관리시스템; 금융 기존 QMS와 통합

question: The bank uses a vendor-provided credit scoring AI as a deployer under the EU AI Act. What are the bank's specific obligations under Article 26 as a deployer of a High-Risk AI system?
ground_truth: Article 26 establishes the specific obligations of deployers (as distinct from providers) of High-Risk AI systems. For the bank as a credit scoring AI deployer: (1) Appropriate use obligation (Art.26(1)) — deploy the High-Risk AI only for its intended purpose, in accordance with instructions for use (Art.13), and following state of the art; (2) Human oversight implementation (Art.26(2)) — assign human oversight to natural persons with the necessary competence, training, and authority to perform it as specified in the instructions for use; (3) Input data relevance (Art.26(3)) — ensure input data used by the High-Risk AI is relevant and sufficiently representative for the AI's intended purpose; (4) Monitoring obligation (Art.26(4)) — monitor the operation of the High-Risk AI system on the basis of instructions for use; if the deployer has reason to believe that use may not be in accordance with the AI Act, inform the provider and suspend use; (5) Significant risk notification (Art.26(5)) — if the deployer identifies a risk that was not previously identified by the provider, notify the provider and relevant market surveillance authority; (6) Logging obligation (Art.26(6)) — keep logs generated by the High-Risk AI system to the extent under its control, for minimum period and in compliance with applicable law; (7) Fundamental rights impact assessment (Art.26(9) and Art.27) — before using High-Risk AI systems for certain purposes (public authority, essential services), conduct a fundamental rights impact assessment; (8) Worker information (Art.26(7)) — inform workers' representatives and affected workers about the use of High-Risk AI in the workplace; (9) The AI Review Committee should verify that all Article 26 obligations are embedded in the bank's vendor contract and operational procedures.
notes: Art.26 배포자 의무; 공급자와 구분되는 은행의 의무

question: Article 27 requires deployers of certain High-Risk AI systems to conduct a Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (FRIA). Does the bank's credit scoring AI trigger this requirement, and what must the assessment cover?
ground_truth: Article 27 requires deployers who are bodies governed by public law, or private entities providing public services (including financial services subject to banking/insurance supervision), to conduct a FRIA before using High-Risk AI systems listed in Annex III. For the bank's credit scoring AI: (1) Triggering condition — Article 27(1) applies to deployers that are: (a) bodies governed by public law; or (b) private entities providing public services; banks providing essential financial services (credit access) may fall under category (b), particularly if they provide services under public service obligations or government-backed programs; the bank should assess whether its credit services constitute public services for FRIA purposes; (2) FRIA mandatory content (Art.27(2)): (a) description of the intended use of the High-Risk AI system; (b) affected geographic area and time period of use; (c) categories of persons and groups likely to be affected; (d) specific risks of harm to those persons; (e) description of implementation of human oversight measures; (f) measures to address identified risks, including monitoring arrangements; (3) Connection to Art.9 RMS — the FRIA supplements the Art.9 risk management system by specifically focusing on fundamental rights impacts rather than just technical risks; (4) Documentation obligation (Art.27(5)) — the FRIA must be submitted to the relevant market surveillance authority upon request; (5) Practical recommendation — even if the FRIA is not technically mandatory for the bank's specific structure, conducting one is best practice and demonstrates comprehensive governance to regulators and customers.
notes: Art.27 기본권 영향평가; 공공 서비스 제공 금융기관

question: Article 15 of the EU AI Act requires High-Risk AI systems to achieve appropriate levels of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity. What specific standards does the AI Review Committee need to apply for the life insurance pricing AI?
ground_truth: Article 15 establishes three performance requirements for High-Risk AI: accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity. For life insurance pricing AI: (1) Accuracy requirements (Art.15(1)) — the AI must achieve an appropriate level of accuracy for its intended purpose; 'appropriate' is contextual: for insurance pricing, accuracy means the pricing model genuinely reflects the individual's actual risk level without systematic error; accuracy must be declared in the Annex IV technical documentation including relevant accuracy metrics; (2) Accuracy throughout lifecycle (Art.15(1)) — accuracy must be maintained throughout the operational lifetime, not just at deployment; requires continuous post-market monitoring (Art.72) to detect accuracy degradation; (3) Cybersecurity requirements (Art.15(1)) — the AI must be resilient against cyberattacks through technical safeguards; for insurance AI, key cybersecurity concerns include: protection against adversarial inputs (applicants manipulating health data inputs); data integrity protection (ensuring pricing inputs are authentic); model inversion attacks (inferring private health information from pricing outputs); (4) Robustness to errors (Art.15(2)) — the AI must be resilient to errors, faults, or inconsistencies in inputs; insurance AI must be designed to fail safely (flag for human review rather than produce erroneous pricing) when input data is incomplete, contradictory, or anomalous; (5) Redundancy and fallback (Art.15(3)) — technical redundancy solutions may be required, appropriate to the risk level; for insurance pricing AI, this may mean maintaining a validated fallback pricing methodology when the AI system is unavailable; (6) Accuracy metrics — the AI Review Committee should require specific accuracy benchmarks (e.g., comparison of AI-predicted vs. actual claims rates) before approval.
notes: Art.15 정확성·강건성·사이버보안; 보험 가격 산정 AI 적용

question: The bank's fraud detection AI is used to identify suspicious transactions in real time. The AI Review Committee asks whether this qualifies as High-Risk AI under the EU AI Act.
ground_truth: Annex III §5(b) of the EU AI Act explicitly exempts fraud detection AI from High-Risk classification. The full provision reads: 'AI systems intended to be used to evaluate the creditworthiness of natural persons or establish their credit score, with the exception of AI systems used for the purpose of detecting financial fraud.' This exception creates an important governance differentiation: (1) Fraud detection AI is NOT High-Risk — the EU legislature made a deliberate policy choice to exclude fraud detection from High-Risk classification, recognizing that: fraud detection AI protects rather than harms financial consumers; real-time fraud detection would be undermined if it required the same extensive documentation and assessment as credit decisions; the potential for harm from fraud detection errors (false positives) is different in character from credit scoring errors; (2) Practical implications for the AI Review Committee — fraud detection AI does not require: Art.9 risk management system; Art.11 technical documentation; Art.14 human oversight mandate; Art.43 conformity assessment; EU database registration; (3) However, lighter-touch governance still applies — even without High-Risk classification, Article 50 transparency obligations may apply if the fraud detection AI interacts with natural persons or generates synthetic content; general EU AI Act obligations (Art.5 prohibited practices check, GDPR compliance) apply; (4) Contrast with credit scoring — the same institution's credit scoring AI IS High-Risk while its fraud detection AI is NOT; the AI Review Committee must apply different governance tracks to these two systems.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Annex III §5(b) exception
notes: 사기탐지 AI의 명시적 High-Risk 제외; 신용평가와 차별화

question: The bank's securities subsidiary uses a robo-advisory AI for portfolio recommendations for retail investors. The AI Review Committee must determine whether this qualifies as High-Risk AI under the EU AI Act.
ground_truth: Robo-advisory AI classification under the EU AI Act requires careful analysis of Annex III: (1) Annex III §5 review — §5(a) covers AI evaluating eligibility for essential public services and benefits; §5(b) covers creditworthiness evaluation and credit scoring; §5(c) covers life and health insurance risk assessment and pricing; §5(d) covers emergency call classification and dispatch; (2) Robo-advisory fit — investment portfolio recommendations do not clearly fit any Annex III §5 category; investment advice is not evaluating creditworthiness (§5(b)) or insurance pricing (§5(c)); (3) Other Annex III categories — §4 covers employment AI; §8 covers judicial AI; none clearly capture investment advisory AI for retail clients; (4) Current assessment — robo-advisory AI for retail investment recommendations does NOT appear to be High-Risk under Annex III as currently written; (5) However, Article 7 allows the European Commission to amend Annex III through delegated acts to add new High-Risk AI categories when AI poses harm to health, safety, or fundamental rights; investment AI could be added in future updates; (6) General-Purpose AI considerations — if the robo-advisory AI is built on a GPAI model (e.g., an LLM), Chapter V GPAI model obligations may apply to the underlying model provider; (7) Article 50 transparency — if the robo-advisory AI interacts with natural persons and could be mistaken for a human, AI disclosure obligations apply; (8) MiFID II suitability requirements independently govern investment AI regardless of EU AI Act classification; (9) AI Review Committee should apply robust governance even without High-Risk classification, given the financial impact on retail investors.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Annex III; Article 6; Article 7
notes: 로보어드바이저 High-Risk 비해당; 향후 확장 가능성

question: Article 72 of the EU AI Act requires providers of High-Risk AI to establish post-market monitoring plans. What must the credit scoring AI's post-market monitoring plan include?
ground_truth: Article 72 establishes post-market monitoring obligations for High-Risk AI providers. The monitoring plan must: (1) Active post-market monitoring system (Art.72(1)) — providers must establish and document a post-market monitoring system that actively collects, documents, and analyzes relevant data on the performance of High-Risk AI throughout its lifetime; (2) Plan content (Art.72(3)) — the post-market monitoring plan must include: (a) intended use cases and relevant performance metrics; (b) evaluation methods for accuracy and performance against stated requirements; (c) monitoring methodology including frequency of evaluations; (d) timeframes for evaluating whether corrective actions are needed; (3) Data collection from deployers (Art.72(4)) — where applicable, the monitoring system must include collection of data provided by deployers; the vendor providing credit scoring AI must therefore establish mechanisms to receive performance feedback from bank clients; (4) Serious incident reporting connection (Art.73) — post-market monitoring feeds directly into the serious incident reporting obligation; if monitoring reveals serious incidents (deaths, serious injuries, rights violations), these must be reported to market surveillance authorities; (5) For credit scoring AI specifically: monitor: model accuracy and performance disaggregated by customer segment; fairness metrics over time (approval rate disparities); customer complaint rates and appeal outcomes; override rates by human loan officers; (6) Review frequency — the plan must specify monitoring frequency; for credit scoring AI with significant fundamental rights impact, quarterly monitoring of key metrics with annual comprehensive review is recommended; (7) The AI Review Committee should review and approve the post-market monitoring plan before deployment.
notes: Art.72 출시 후 모니터링; 신용 AI 성능 지속 감시

question: Article 73 of the EU AI Act requires reporting of serious incidents involving High-Risk AI. What constitutes a 'serious incident' for a credit scoring AI, and what is the reporting process?
ground_truth: Article 73 requires providers of High-Risk AI to report any serious incident to the market surveillance authorities of Member States where the incident occurred. Key elements: (1) Definition of serious incident (Art.3 definition) — 'serious incident' means an incident or malfunctioning of an AI system that directly or indirectly leads to: (a) death of a person or serious damage to health; (b) disruption of the management and operation of critical infrastructure; (c) violation of obligations under Union law protecting fundamental rights; (d) serious damage to property or the environment; (2) Application to credit scoring AI — a serious incident for credit scoring AI could include: a systematic bias error leading to discriminatory denial of credit at scale, potentially violating fundamental rights protected under EU non-discrimination law; a data breach through the AI system exposing applicants' personal and financial data; a major system failure leading to incorrect credit decisions affecting significant numbers of applicants; (3) Reporting timeline (Art.73(3)) — immediate notification: without undue delay and no later than 72 hours after becoming aware of the serious incident for incidents involving death or violations of obligations protecting fundamental rights; other incidents: as soon as possible; (4) Reporting obligations — providers report to market surveillance authorities; deployers (banks) who become aware of serious incidents must also notify providers immediately; (5) AI Review Committee role — establish an AI serious incident detection and reporting procedure that: identifies what constitutes a serious incident for each High-Risk AI; defines the reporting chain (AI ops team → committee → regulatory reporting); ensures 72-hour reporting timeline can be met; (6) Distinction from standard monitoring — serious incidents trigger regulatory reporting; performance degradation within normal ranges is handled through standard post-market monitoring.
notes: Art.73 중대 사고 보고; 72시간 보고 의무

question: The bank internally develops a credit scoring AI system. Does Article 43 of the EU AI Act require external conformity assessment, or can the bank conduct self-assessment?
ground_truth: Article 43 establishes the conformity assessment framework for High-Risk AI systems. For credit scoring AI: (1) Default conformity assessment procedure — for High-Risk AI listed in Annex III (which includes credit scoring under §5(b)), Article 43(2) specifies that providers shall follow the conformity assessment procedure set out in Annex VI (internal control) or, at the provider's choice, in Annex VII (third-party quality management system assessment); (2) Self-assessment availability (Annex VI) — for most Annex III High-Risk AI systems (excluding those in Annex III points 1, 6, and 7 which require third-party notified body assessment), providers may conduct conformity assessment through internal control (self-assessment); credit scoring AI under Annex III §5(b) is eligible for self-assessment under Annex VI; (3) Annex VI self-assessment content — the internal control procedure requires: verification that the technical documentation is complete and up to date; verification that the AI system complies with Chapter III Section 2 requirements; implementation and maintenance of a QMS per Art.17; (4) EU Declaration of Conformity (Art.47) — after completing conformity assessment, the provider must draw up an EU Declaration of Conformity confirming compliance with all applicable requirements; (5) CE Marking (Art.48) — the credit scoring AI must bear CE marking after successful conformity assessment; (6) Registration (Art.49) — the provider must register the High-Risk AI in the EU database for High-Risk AI systems; (7) AI Review Committee obligation — verify that conformity assessment is completed before any EU deployment; self-assessment documents must be available for regulatory review.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Article 43; Annex VI
notes: Art.43 적합성 평가; 신용 AI는 자체 평가 가능

question: Article 50 of the EU AI Act establishes transparency obligations for providers and deployers of certain AI systems. How does Article 50 apply to the bank's customer service chatbot and its generative AI features?
ground_truth: Article 50 establishes transparency obligations that apply to AI systems beyond just High-Risk AI. For the bank's customer service chatbot with generative AI features: (1) AI interaction disclosure (Art.50(1)) — providers of AI systems intended to interact directly with natural persons must design systems that inform natural persons that they are interacting with an AI, except when evident from context; for a banking chatbot, this means: a clear disclosure at the start of each interaction that the customer is talking with an AI system; the disclosure must be meaningful, not just a buried footer; (2) Deep fake and synthetic content marking (Art.50(2)) — providers of AI systems generating synthetic audio, image, video, or text that may falsely appear to be human-generated must mark outputs as artificially generated; for text-based chatbots, mark AI-generated financial advice as AI-generated; (3) Emotion recognition and biometric AI disclosure (Art.50(3)) — operators using AI for emotion recognition or biometric categorization must inform natural persons; (4) Copyright and style mimicry exception (Art.50(3)) — AI-generated content for artistic purposes may be excepted from marking requirements; not applicable to financial service chatbots; (5) Deployer obligation (Art.50(4)) — deployers using AI to generate or manipulate content that might appear genuine must disclose the AI-generated nature, except for legitimate law enforcement, safety, or authorized testing purposes; (6) Failure to disclose is not explicitly penalized under Article 50 in the same way as prohibited practices, but national competent authorities can take enforcement action; (7) Integration with AI기본법 §31 — Article 50 parallels Korean AI Basic Act §31 disclosure obligations; simultaneous compliance is achievable through a unified disclosure policy.
notes: Art.50 투명성 의무; 챗봇 AI 고지

question: Chapter VII of the EU AI Act establishes a governance structure including the AI Office, European AI Board, and national competent authorities. How does this governance structure affect financial institutions' AI Review Committees?
ground_truth: Chapter VII of the EU AI Act establishes a multi-layered governance structure that creates the regulatory environment within which financial institutions' AI Review Committees must operate: (1) AI Office (Art.64) — the European Commission's AI Office provides oversight of GPAI model providers, coordinates AI Act implementation, and develops guidelines; financial AI using GPAI models (e.g., LLMs for customer service) is subject to AI Office oversight of the underlying model provider; (2) European AI Board (Art.65-66) — composed of national competent authority representatives; issues opinions and guidance on AI Act interpretation; coordinates enforcement; financial institutions should monitor AI Board opinions for guidance on financial AI classification and compliance; (3) National Competent Authorities (Art.70) — each EU Member State designates a national competent authority responsible for AI Act implementation and market surveillance; for financial institutions, the competent authority for financial AI may be the national financial regulator (e.g., BaFin in Germany, AMF in France) or a cross-sectoral AI authority; (4) Scientific Panel (Art.68) — independent experts supporting AI Office in GPAI model evaluation; (5) Advisory Forum (Art.67) — stakeholder representation; financial institutions can participate through industry associations; (6) Impact on AI Review Committee: monitor AI Board and national authority guidance as it develops; engage with financial regulators who may be designated competent authorities; participate in industry responses to AI Board consultations; understand which authority will conduct market surveillance of financial AI in each EU country of operation; be prepared for market surveillance investigations and inspections per Chapter IX.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Chapter VII; Articles 64-70
notes: EU AI Act 거버넌스 구조; 금융 AI 규제 당국 관계

question: The bank uses a large language model (LLM) from a major AI company as the foundation for its customer advisory chatbot and credit decision explanation generator. How does Chapter V of the EU AI Act (General-Purpose AI Models) affect the bank's governance obligations?
ground_truth: Chapter V of the EU AI Act governs General-Purpose AI Models (GPAI models) — AI models trained on large amounts of data that can perform a wide range of tasks. Key provisions affecting the bank: (1) GPAI model vs GPAI system distinction — the LLM itself is a GPAI model; the bank's credit explanation chatbot built on top of the LLM is an AI system using a GPAI model; (2) GPAI provider obligations (Art.53) — the AI company providing the LLM (not the bank) must: prepare and maintain technical documentation; provide information to downstream providers; publish a sufficiently detailed summary of training data; comply with copyright law; maintain model documentation; (3) Bank's obligations as downstream deployer — the bank building applications on the LLM must: assess whether the resulting AI systems are High-Risk (the credit explanation generator using a GPAI model may be High-Risk if it makes or significantly influences credit decisions); implement appropriate governance for any High-Risk AI system built on the GPAI; (4) Systemic risk GPAI (Art.51) — LLMs above the 10^25 FLOP training threshold are classified as GPAI with systemic risk; such models face additional obligations including adversarial testing; the AI company's compliance affects whether their LLM can be legally deployed in the EU; (5) AI Review Committee obligation — verify that the LLM provider complies with Chapter V obligations; request access to provider's technical documentation and training data summary; assess whether chatbot applications built on the LLM become High-Risk AI under Article 6; document the GPAI model used in the technical documentation of any High-Risk AI systems built upon it.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Chapter V; Articles 51-56
notes: GPAI 모델과 금융 AI; LLM 기반 챗봇 거버넌스

question: Article 5 of the EU AI Act prohibits certain AI practices absolutely. The AI Review Committee must screen all proposed financial AI systems for prohibited practices. What Article 5 prohibitions are most relevant to financial AI?
ground_truth: Article 5 establishes absolute prohibitions on AI practices that the EU considers incompatible with EU values. Prohibitions most relevant to financial AI: (1) Social scoring by public authorities (Art.5(1)(c)) — prohibits AI systems evaluating or classifying natural persons based on social behavior or personality characteristics in ways that cause unjustified or disproportionate detrimental treatment; while this prohibition specifically targets public authorities, private financial institutions should note that comprehensive financial scoring systems approaching 'social scoring' may face regulatory scrutiny; (2) Emotion recognition in workplaces and education (Art.5(1)(f)) — prohibits AI systems for emotion recognition in workplace and educational settings; if the bank uses AI emotion recognition for employee performance assessment or customer interaction analysis, this is prohibited; (3) Real-time biometric identification in public spaces (Art.5(1)(h)) — prohibits remote real-time biometric identification in public spaces for law enforcement; not typically relevant to standard banking AI but relevant to any physical security AI deployed in bank branches; (4) Manipulation and exploitation (Art.5(1)(a)) — prohibits AI using subliminal techniques to manipulate behavior or exploit vulnerabilities to distort behavior; financial AI using dark patterns or exploiting vulnerable customers' cognitive limitations to push unsuitable products may approach this prohibition; (5) AI Review Committee screening obligation — before any AI system is approved, the committee must explicitly confirm it does not fall within any Article 5 prohibition; document the prohibition screening as part of the approval record; the AI Act's prohibited practices have been applicable since February 2025 (Art.113(1)).
notes: Art.5 금지 AI 관행; 금융 AI에 적용되는 금지 사항

question: The bank (as deployer of a vendor credit scoring AI) discovers that the AI is producing unexpectedly poor outputs for a specific customer segment. What does Article 26 require the bank to do?
ground_truth: Articles 26(4) and 26(5) establish the deployer's obligations when problems with High-Risk AI are detected: (1) Article 26(4) — monitoring and suspension obligation: deployers must monitor the operation of the High-Risk AI system on the basis of instructions for use; if the deployer has reason to believe that the use of the High-Risk AI in accordance with its instructions is not compliant with the AI Act (including due to poor outputs for a specific customer segment suggesting bias or inaccuracy), the deployer must: inform the provider or distributor without undue delay; suspend use of the system where relevant; (2) Article 26(5) — serious risk notification: if the deployer identifies a serious risk to health, safety, or fundamental rights, they must immediately inform the provider of the AI system AND the relevant market surveillance authority in the Member State where the deployer is established; (3) What 'reason to believe' non-compliance means for the credit AI scenario: unexpectedly poor outputs for a specific customer segment could indicate: data representation failure (the model's training data did not adequately represent this segment); systematic bias that was not detected before deployment; model drift affecting certain segments disproportionately; (4) Suspension threshold — Article 26(4) says 'where relevant'; the bank must assess whether the issue poses sufficient risk to justify suspension; for a fundamental rights issue like discriminatory credit outcomes, suspension of AI-driven decisions for the affected segment pending investigation is appropriate; (5) Communication to affected customers — while not explicitly required by Art.26, the fundamental rights implications of systematic bias in credit decisions warrant proactive customer communication; (6) The AI Review Committee should ensure this monitoring and notification process is documented in advance.
notes: Art.26 모니터링 및 사용 중단; 배포자의 사전·사후 의무

question: Article 99 of the EU AI Act establishes penalties for non-compliance. What are the penalty levels for financial institutions that violate High-Risk AI obligations, and how should the AI Review Committee factor these into governance prioritization?
ground_truth: Article 99 establishes a tiered penalty structure for EU AI Act violations: (1) Tier 1 — prohibited practices (Art.5): up to €35 million or 7% of total worldwide annual turnover (whichever is higher); for a large Korean bank, 7% of global turnover could be billions; these are the most severe penalties and apply to absolute prohibitions; (2) Tier 2 — High-Risk AI non-compliance (Chapter III obligations, Chapter V GPAI obligations, Art.13 transparency, Art.26 deployer obligations): up to €15 million or 3% of total worldwide annual turnover; this tier covers the bulk of financial AI governance obligations; (3) Tier 3 — provision of incorrect information (Art.86, Art.91): up to €7.5 million or 1% of total worldwide annual turnover; (4) SME and startup moderation — for SMEs and startups, national competent authorities may impose reduced penalties proportionate to size; (5) Governance prioritization implications for the AI Review Committee: the penalty calculation based on worldwide turnover means large global banks face potentially massive penalties for seemingly local EU AI Act violations; prioritize zero-tolerance compliance for: Article 5 prohibited practices (Tier 1 — existential penalty risk); Article 9 risk management, Article 14 human oversight, Article 26 deployer obligations (Tier 2 — material penalty risk); high-risk AI registration and technical documentation; (6) Penalty assessment factors — national authorities consider: nature, gravity, and duration; whether harm was intentional or negligent; mitigating actions; financial capacity; (7) The penalty structure makes EU AI Act compliance economically rational even for institutions that treat compliance as a cost-benefit calculation.
notes: Art.99 제재; 전 세계 매출 기반 과징금

question: How should the AI Review Committee plan for future changes to the EU AI Act, including potential expansions of the High-Risk AI list under Article 7?
ground_truth: The EU AI Act is designed to evolve with AI technology. Key mechanisms the AI Review Committee must monitor: (1) Article 7 — Amendments to Annex III: the European Commission may amend Annex III through delegated acts to add or modify High-Risk AI categories when AI systems pose sufficient risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights; financial AI categories that could be added include: investment advisory AI; comprehensive customer financial scoring AI; AI used in systemic risk management; (2) Article 112 — Evaluation and Review: by August 2030 (5 years after full application), the Commission must evaluate and report to Parliament on the functioning of the AI Act, including whether to amend the High-Risk AI classification criteria; this establishes a formal regulatory evolution cycle; (3) Delegated and implementing acts — numerous provisions authorize Commission delegated acts and implementing acts that will fill in technical details; monitor Official Journal publications; (4) Technical standards — harmonized technical standards developed by CEN/CENELEC and other standardization bodies will operationalize many vague AI Act requirements; monitor standards development and adopt standards as they are published; (5) AI Office guidelines (Art.96) — the Commission will issue guidelines on AI Act implementation; for financial AI, these guidelines may address specific questions about credit scoring, insurance pricing, and investment AI; (6) AI Review Committee forward planning — conduct an annual regulatory horizon scan; maintain a tracker of delegated acts, implementing acts, and Annex III amendment proposals; build governance processes that can accommodate regulatory evolution without requiring fundamental restructuring.
notes: EU AI Act 진화; Annex III 확장 모니터링

question: Article 6(3) of the EU AI Act allows providers to argue that an Annex III AI system is not actually High-Risk because it does not pose significant risk. Can the bank argue that its credit scoring AI is not High-Risk despite being listed in Annex III §5(b)?
ground_truth: Article 6(3) creates an exception to Annex III High-Risk classification: an AI system that is listed in Annex III shall NOT be considered High-Risk if it does not pose a significant risk of harm to health, safety, or the fundamental rights of natural persons. However, this exception has narrow applicability: (1) Exception criteria (Art.6(3)) — the AI system is not High-Risk if: (a) it performs a narrow procedural task; (b) it improves the result of a previously completed human activity; (c) it detects decision-making patterns or deviations from prior decision-making patterns and is not meant to replace or influence the previously completed human assessment without proper human review; or (d) it performs a preparatory task to an assessment relevant for the purpose listed in Annex III; (2) Application to credit scoring AI — a credit scoring AI that makes or directly influences credit approval/rejection decisions does NOT qualify for this exception; it is not merely performing a narrow procedural task or preparing for human assessment; it is making substantive judgments that directly affect credit access; (3) Narrow exception scope — the exception is designed for AI systems that assist in administrative tasks related to Annex III activities, not for AI that is the primary decision mechanism; (4) Unjustified non-High-Risk claims — Article 80 addresses situations where providers unjustifiably classify Annex III AI as non-High-Risk; market surveillance authorities can investigate and require High-Risk compliance; (5) Documentation requirement — if the bank's credit AI were to invoke Art.6(3), it must document the reasoning and notify the market surveillance authority per Art.49(2); the AI Review Committee should not invoke Art.6(3) for credit scoring AI.
notes: Art.6(3) 비고위험 예외; 신용 AI에 적용 불가 이유

question: The bank's AML AI system screens transactions and customer profiles for money laundering indicators. The AI Review Committee must determine whether this qualifies as High-Risk AI under the EU AI Act's Annex III.
ground_truth: AML AI classification requires careful analysis of Annex III categories: (1) Annex III §6 — Law Enforcement: §6(a) covers AI assessing the risk of a natural person becoming a victim of criminal offences; §6(d) covers AI for assessing risk of a person offending or re-offending; §6(e) covers AI for profiling natural persons during criminal investigation; these provisions are explicitly limited to use 'by or on behalf of law enforcement authorities' or 'Union institutions in support of law enforcement'; (2) Private bank AML AI — a bank's internal AML monitoring AI is not operated 'by or on behalf of law enforcement authorities'; it operates for regulatory compliance (Anti-Money Laundering Directive compliance) and internal fraud prevention; it is not a law enforcement tool; therefore, Annex III §6 does not classify private bank AML AI as High-Risk; (3) Other Annex III categories — AML AI does not clearly fit other Annex III categories (it is not credit scoring, insurance pricing, employment AI, etc.); (4) Current assessment — private bank AML transaction monitoring AI is NOT High-Risk under Annex III as currently written; (5) Article 50 transparency — AML AI does not typically interact directly with customers in a way triggering Art.50; however, if the AI generates customer-facing communications about account restrictions, disclosure obligations may apply; (6) Independent financial regulatory obligations — AML AI compliance is primarily governed by AMLD6/Anti-Money Laundering Regulation rather than EU AI Act; (7) AI Review Committee approach — apply robust governance despite non-High-Risk classification given the significant impact on customer account access rights.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Annex III; Article 6
notes: AML AI 분류; 법집행 목적 AI와 구분

subcategory: EU AI Act and Environmental Considerations
question: The bank's ESG team asks whether the EU AI Act includes any provisions addressing the environmental impact of AI systems. How should the AI Review Committee respond?
ground_truth: The EU AI Act does not include explicit mandatory provisions on AI environmental impact or energy consumption comparable to its human rights and safety requirements. However: (1) Recital considerations — some EU AI Act recitals reference sustainability and environmental considerations as context for the regulation; these do not create binding obligations but signal regulatory direction; (2) Potential connection to Article 9 risk management — the Article 9(1) risk management system broadly covers 'risks to health, safety or fundamental rights'; environmental harm from AI (e.g., massive energy consumption contributing to climate risk) could theoretically be included in a comprehensive risk management framework, though this is not explicitly required; (3) Technical documentation (Art.11, Annex IV) — the technical documentation requirements do not explicitly require energy consumption disclosure, unlike some proposed AI reporting frameworks; (4) EU regulatory evolution — the European Commission has committed to monitoring AI's environmental impact; future amendments to the AI Act or complementary regulations (AI Liability Directive, ESG reporting frameworks) may introduce environmental AI obligations; (5) CSRD connection — banks subject to the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) must report on climate-related risks and impacts; AI energy consumption may need to be included in this reporting even if not required by the AI Act directly; (6) AI Review Committee practical response — while not an EU AI Act requirement, including AI environmental impact assessment in governance as a voluntary best practice: satisfies CSRD reporting needs; demonstrates comprehensive ESG commitment; aligns with NIST AI RMF MEASURE 2.12 environmental impact assessment requirement.
notes: EU AI Act 환경 조항 부재; CSRD와 연계

question: The AI Review Committee must understand the EU AI Act's timeline for applicability. What is the phased implementation schedule under Article 113, and what obligations are already in force for financial institutions?
ground_truth: Article 113 of the EU AI Act establishes a phased implementation timeline: (1) Entry into force — the Regulation entered into force on 1 August 2024 (20 days after publication in the Official Journal on 12 July 2024); (2) Phase 1 — 6 months after entry into force (February 2025): Chapter I (general provisions) and Chapter II (prohibited AI practices, Art.5) become applicable; financial institutions must immediately comply with Article 5 prohibited AI practice prohibitions as of February 2025; (3) Phase 2 — 12 months (August 2025): Chapter V (General-Purpose AI Models) and Chapter VII (Governance) become applicable; GPAI model obligations and EU governance structure are operational; (4) Phase 3 — 24 months (August 2026): the majority of the Regulation becomes applicable, including: Chapter III (High-Risk AI requirements — Art.9-15, 16-27); Chapter IV (Transparency obligations — Art.50); Chapter VIII (EU database); Chapter IX (Post-market monitoring, market surveillance); this is the critical phase for financial AI governance; (5) Phase 4 — 36 months (August 2027): Annex I (harmonized Union legislation) High-Risk AI becomes applicable; (6) For financial institutions (as of May 2026 — current date): Art.5 prohibited practices are already applicable (since February 2025); GPAI model obligations are applicable (since August 2025); High-Risk AI Chapter III obligations are applicable (since August 2026); (7) AI Review Committee priority — all High-Risk financial AI (credit scoring, insurance pricing) must be fully compliant with Chapter III requirements NOW (as of August 2026 application date).
notes: EU AI Act 단계적 시행; 현재 시점(2026.5) 기준 의무 적용 현황

question: Article 26(7) of the EU AI Act requires deployers of High-Risk AI to inform workers and their representatives about the use of High-Risk AI in the workplace. How does this apply to loan officers using the credit scoring AI?
ground_truth: Article 26(7) requires deployers to inform workers and worker representatives about the use of High-Risk AI systems in the workplace before such use begins. For loan officers using the credit scoring AI: (1) Scope — the obligation applies when the High-Risk AI is used to assist or replace human decision-making in work processes; using credit scoring AI to inform loan officer credit decisions is precisely this type of use; (2) What information must be provided — while the Act does not specify exact content, workers must be informed that High-Risk AI is being used and in what capacity; information should include: that the AI is classified as High-Risk under the EU AI Act; the purpose and capabilities of the credit scoring AI; how the AI output is intended to be used in the loan decision process; the human oversight role that loan officers are expected to perform; how loan officers can override or disregard AI recommendations; the monitoring and logging that occurs during AI use; (3) Timing — information must be provided before use begins; for new loan officers, this should be part of onboarding; for existing officers when the system is introduced, advance notice; (4) Workers' representatives — European Works Councils (EWCs) and national workers' councils must be informed for larger institutions; (5) Connection to Art.14 human oversight — worker information under Art.26(7) is foundational to the effective human oversight required by Art.14; loan officers cannot perform meaningful oversight of an AI system they were never informed about; (6) AI Review Committee obligation — ensure Art.26(7) worker information procedure is documented and implemented before deployment.
notes: Art.26(7) 근로자 정보 제공; 대출 담당자 AI 사용 고지

question: Article 15(3) of the EU AI Act requires technical robustness solutions including redundancy for High-Risk AI. How should the AI Review Committee implement cybersecurity and resilience measures for the credit scoring AI consistent with Article 15?
ground_truth: Article 15 requires High-Risk AI systems to achieve appropriate levels of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity throughout their lifecycle, maintained through state-of-the-art measures. Specific cybersecurity requirements for credit scoring AI: (1) Resilience against attempted manipulation (Art.15(1)) — the credit scoring AI must be resilient to attempts by third parties to alter its use, outputs, or performance through manipulation of the AI system, its components, or training data; key threats to address: adversarial input attacks — applicants crafting applications to game the AI scoring system; data poisoning — corrupting training data pipelines to bias future model outputs; prompt injection — for LLM-based components, injecting instructions that alter AI behavior; model extraction — reverse-engineering the scoring model through API access patterns; (2) Resilience against errors and faults (Art.15(2)) — the AI must be designed to be resilient to input errors, faults, or inconsistencies; fail-safe design: when inputs are anomalous or inconsistent (e.g., contradictory financial data), the system should flag for human review rather than producing a potentially erroneous score; (3) Redundancy solutions (Art.15(3)) — technical redundancy may be required appropriate to the risk; for credit scoring AI: hot standby scoring capability to maintain continuity if primary system fails; validated fallback scoring methodology (e.g., rule-based scorecard) for use during AI system unavailability; (4) DORA intersection — EU financial institutions are also subject to the Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA); DORA ICT risk management requirements and EU AI Act Art.15 cybersecurity requirements overlap substantially; the AI Review Committee should integrate compliance to avoid duplication.
notes: Art.15 사이버보안 요건; DORA와 연계

question: Article 9(2)(b) of the EU AI Act requires risk assessment under conditions of 'reasonably foreseeable misuse.' What foreseeable misuse scenarios must the AI Review Committee assess for the credit scoring AI?
ground_truth: Article 9(2)(b) requires estimation and evaluation of risks that may emerge when the High-Risk AI system is used under conditions of 'reasonably foreseeable misuse.' For credit scoring AI, reasonably foreseeable misuse scenarios include: (1) Gaming by applicants — applicants deliberately provide false information or manipulate the data they supply to improve their credit score; the AI should be robust to common gaming strategies (overstating income, disguising debt obligations); assess the model's vulnerability to such manipulation and implement validation checks; (2) Discriminatory use by deployer — the bank deploys the AI specifically targeting or excluding protected demographic groups, using the AI to rationalize discriminatory practices that the AI itself may not explicitly exhibit; the risk management system must include governance controls preventing discriminatory deployment even if the model itself is technically fair; (3) Use beyond intended purpose — using the credit scoring AI to assess creditworthiness for products it was not trained for (e.g., using a consumer credit AI for corporate lending); document the intended use boundaries and implement technical controls preventing out-of-scope use; (4) Overreliance without oversight — loan officers effectively outsourcing all credit judgment to the AI without meaningful oversight (automation bias); the risk management plan must address this specific form of misuse by designing oversight processes that maintain human judgment quality; (5) Secondary use of scoring data — using credit score outputs or model features for purposes beyond credit decisions (e.g., for marketing targeting, customer segmentation for other products) without appropriate consent; (6) Delayed update — continuing to use the AI after market conditions have changed materially, making the model's training data stale; (7) Document each misuse scenario and the corresponding mitigation in the Art.9 risk management system.
notes: 합리적 예측 가능한 오용 평가; 신용 AI 시나리오

question: Article 4 of the EU AI Act requires providers and deployers to take measures ensuring sufficient AI literacy of their staff. What AI literacy obligations does this create for the bank's AI Review Committee?
ground_truth: Article 4 of the EU AI Act requires providers and deployers of AI systems to take measures to ensure, to their best extent, a sufficient level of AI literacy of their staff and other persons dealing with the operation and use of AI systems on their behalf, taking into account their technical knowledge, experience, education, and training context, as well as considering the users the AI systems are intended to be used by. For the bank's AI Review Committee: (1) Scope of obligation — applies to all staff dealing with AI operation and use; for a bank this includes: AI developers and data scientists (deep technical literacy); AI Review Committee members (governance and risk literacy); loan officers and other business users of AI (operational literacy); compliance and legal staff (regulatory literacy); board members (strategic literacy); (2) Literacy content should be calibrated by role: technical staff — AI methods, model validation, bias detection; business users — AI capabilities and limitations, when to override, how to interpret outputs; governance staff — EU AI Act requirements, risk management frameworks, FRIA process; executives — AI governance principles, strategic risk implications; (3) Proportionality — 'to their best extent' and 'taking into account technical knowledge' allow proportionate implementation; a loan officer does not need to understand model architecture; (4) Connection to Art.14 human oversight — AI literacy is the foundation for effective human oversight; loan officers who lack literacy about the credit AI's capabilities and limitations cannot perform meaningful oversight; (5) Training program design — the AI Review Committee should develop a tiered AI literacy curriculum covering EU AI Act obligations, AI capabilities and limitations specific to each system, and governance procedures; (6) Record-keeping — maintain training completion records as evidence of Art.4 compliance.
notes: Art.4 AI 리터러시; 역할별 차등 교육

question: The bank's AI customer service chatbot is deployed in Germany to answer questions about banking products. Article 50(1) requires disclosure that the customer is interacting with an AI. What are the specific requirements and exceptions?
ground_truth: Article 50(1) requires providers of AI systems intended to interact directly with natural persons to design and develop the AI so that the natural persons are informed they are interacting with an AI system, in a timely, clear, and intelligible manner, and at the latest at the beginning of the first interaction. However, exceptions exist: (1) Evident from context exception — the disclosure obligation does not apply where it is evident from the context and the circumstances that the natural person is interacting with an AI system; if the bank's chatbot is clearly branded as 'AI Assistant' or accessed through a clearly labeled AI channel, the disclosure may be self-evident; (2) Law enforcement exception — disclosure may be waived where the AI system is authorized by law for detecting, preventing, investigating, or prosecuting crime; not applicable to banking chatbots; (3) Specific requirements: timing — disclosure must occur 'at the latest at the beginning of the first interaction' (not buried in terms and conditions or disclosed later); clarity — the disclosure must be clear and intelligible to the average user, not just technically present; (4) Practical implementation for banking chatbot: opening message should state explicitly 'You are now chatting with an AI assistant'; if the chat includes human handoff, notify when transitioning from AI to human and back; branding and interface design should reinforce the AI nature of the interaction; (5) Contrast with Article 50(2) — Art.50(1) covers interaction disclosure; Art.50(2) covers marking of AI-generated content; both may apply to the banking chatbot; (6) Enforcement — national competent authorities in Germany (BaFin or AI-specific authority) may investigate and penalize violations.
notes: Art.50(1) 챗봇 AI 고지; 첫 상호작용 시 의무

question: Article 49 of the EU AI Act requires registration of High-Risk AI systems in an EU database. What are the registration requirements for the bank's credit scoring AI, and who is responsible?
ground_truth: Article 49 requires registration of High-Risk AI systems listed in Annex III in the EU database established under Article 71. Registration requirements: (1) Provider registration obligation — before placing a High-Risk AI system on the EU market or putting it into service, the provider (not the deployer) must register the system in the EU database; for credit scoring AI, the provider (bank if internally developed; vendor if using external system) must register; (2) Registration content (Art.49(1)) — providers must submit information including: provider identity; High-Risk AI category under Annex III; AI system description, intended purpose, and main capabilities; system version information; EU Declaration of Conformity; contact information; countries where the system is or will be deployed; (3) Deployer registration for Annex III §2 (critical infrastructure) and §8 (justice/democracy) — deployers of these specific categories must also separately register their use; credit scoring AI under Annex III §5(b) does not appear to require additional deployer registration; (4) Database public access (Art.71) — the EU database is publicly accessible; registered AI systems are visible to the public; this creates transparency about what High-Risk AI is in use across the EU; (5) Update obligation — providers must update registration information when significant changes occur to the AI system; (6) For the bank as deployer (using vendor credit scoring AI): verify the vendor has registered the system before deploying in the EU; request registration confirmation and database entry identifier from the vendor; (7) AI Review Committee obligation — confirm EU database registration as a prerequisite for EU deployment approval.
notes: Art.49 EU 데이터베이스 등록; 공급자 의무

question: After implementing all feasible risk mitigations for the life insurance pricing AI, some residual risk of inaccurate pricing remains for rare medical conditions. Article 9(5) requires residual risk to be acceptable. How should the AI Review Committee determine acceptability?
ground_truth: Article 9(5) requires that risk management measures ensure: (a) the relevant residual risk associated with each hazard is judged to be acceptable; and (b) the overall residual risk of the High-Risk AI system is judged to be acceptable. The three-step hierarchy for residual risk acceptability: (1) Step 1 — Elimination or reduction of risks as far as technically feasible through design (Art.9(5)(a)) — for insurance pricing inaccuracy for rare conditions: attempt to expand training data for rare conditions; use transfer learning from related conditions; implement conservative pricing bounds for low-confidence predictions; document why further reduction is not technically feasible; (2) Step 2 — Implementation of adequate mitigation and control measures (Art.9(5)(b)) — for residual inaccuracy: mandatory human underwriter review for all applications involving rare medical conditions; human override authority for pricing decisions above certain thresholds; enhanced monitoring of claims outcomes for rare condition policyholders; (3) Step 3 — Provision of information and training to deployers (Art.9(5)(c)) — inform the insurance underwriting team of: the AI system's known limitations for rare conditions; the specific conditions and scenarios where accuracy is lower; the mandatory human review process; (4) Acceptability determination — 'acceptable' residual risk is not defined in the Act; the provider must make a documented judgment considering: magnitude of potential harm (inaccurate pricing causing inability to access insurance); likelihood of occurrence; availability of alternatives (human-only underwriting for rare conditions); whether affected persons have meaningful recourse (human review, appeal); (5) Documentation — the residual risk acceptability judgment must be documented in the Art.9 risk management system and referenced in the technical documentation; (6) The AI Review Committee must formally approve the residual risk acceptability determination as part of deployment approval.
notes: Art.9(5) 잔여 리스크 수용 가능성; 단계적 위험 완화

question: How does the EU AI Act's requirement for management accountability (Article 17(1)(j)) translate into board-level reporting requirements for financial institutions?
ground_truth: Article 17(1)(j) requires the Quality Management System to cover the 'responsibilities of management' within the organization.
